content.text: COLLATERAL INFORMATION
Client: Alex Rivera (they/them) | DOB: 1997-11-03
Compiled for clinical records
Confidential
PARTNER OBSERVATIONS — Jordan Mitchell
Collateral interview conducted with client's written consent on August 20, 2025.
Jordan Mitchell (he/him), age 29, partner of 3 years, provided the following observations:

Sleep: 'Alex tosses and turns all night. They wake up gasping or sometimes crying out. I've started sleeping in the guest room some nights because I can't sleep either, and I feel terrible about it. When they do sleep, it's restless — they grind their teeth and sometimes kick. Before the accident, Alex was a deep sleeper. We used to joke that nothing could wake them.'

Driving avoidance: 'Alex hasn't driven since the accident. Not once. I drive everywhere, or they take the bus or Lyft. They won't even sit in the front seat sometimes — they say it feels too exposed. When I'm driving and a truck passes us, Alex grips the door handle and closes their eyes. One time on the highway a truck merged into our lane and Alex screamed and started shaking. I had to pull over and they were confused about where they were for a minute. That scared me.'

Emotional withdrawal: 'The Alex I knew was warm, funny, creative — they'd show me their designs and we'd talk for hours. Now it's like they're behind glass. They come home from work and just sit there. They don't draw anymore, which is a huge red flag because art was their whole identity. They get irritated over small things — I asked them to take out the trash once and they snapped at me. Then they apologized for like 20 minutes. They've stopped wanting to see friends, go out, do anything we used to enjoy together. Our intimate life has basically stopped — they say they feel numb and can't be close to anyone. I love them and I want to support them but I'm exhausted and honestly scared for them.'

Alcohol concern: 'The drinking worries me. Before the accident, Alex would have a beer or two on weekends. Now they drink every night. They say it helps them sleep, and I get it, but some nights it's four or five beers and they fall asleep on the couch. I've tried bringing it up and they get defensive. I don't want to be controlling but Alex's dad had a drinking problem and I can see the pattern.'

Positive observations: 'Alex is trying. They started therapy and they do the homework. Some weeks I can see small improvements — they'll make a joke, or they'll come out of their studio and show me something they worked on. Those moments give me hope. I just want them back — or some version of them that isn't in so much pain.'

AUTO INSURANCE INDEPENDENT MEDICAL EXAMINATION (IME) — SUMMARY

Independent Medical Examination Report — Summary for Clinical Records
Examiner: Dr. Richard Patel, MD, Board-Certified Psychiatrist
Insurer: Nationwide Auto Insurance (Policy #NAI-8827451)
Date of Examination: September 22, 2025
Claimant: Alex Rivera

Reason for IME: Assessment of psychiatric disability related to MVA claim (November 15, 2024). Requested by insurer to evaluate current psychiatric status, treatment necessity, and functional capacity.

Findings:
Dr. Patel conducted a 90-minute clinical interview and reviewed available medical records (emergency department records, PCP notes, physical therapy records, and the intake assessment from Lakeshore Behavioral Health Center). Standardized assessment instruments were administered (PCL-5, PHQ-9, BDI-II).

Diagnostic Impressions: Dr. Patel concurred with diagnoses of PTSD (F43.10) and Major Depressive Disorder (F32.1) causally related to the MVA. He noted that GAD (F41.1) 'may represent a pre-existing vulnerability that was sub-clinical prior to the accident and has been activated and exacerbated by the traumatic event.' He deferred on AUD diagnosis, noting 'alcohol use is elevated and concerning but appears secondary to PTSD and may remit with effective trauma treatment.'

Functional Assessment: Dr. Patel assessed Alex as having moderate psychiatric disability affecting occupational, social, and daily functioning. He estimated current functional capacity at approximately 70% of pre-MVA baseline, with primary limitations in:
- Driving and transportation (severe limitation)
- Sustained concentration for complex creative work (moderate limitation)
- Interpersonal engagement and social participation (moderate limitation)
- Sleep quality and daytime energy (moderate-severe limitation)

Treatment Recommendations: Dr. Patel endorsed continued CPT as appropriate evidence-based treatment. He recommended a 12-16 session course with reassessment thereafter. He noted that current medication management (sertraline 100mg, trazodone 50mg) is reasonable and that additional pharmacological intervention (specifically prazosin for nightmares) should be considered if sleep does not improve with therapy.

Prognosis: 'Given Alex Rivera's age, pre-morbid functioning level, absence of prior PTSD, engagement in evidence-based treatment, and strong psychosocial supports, prognosis for meaningful recovery is good. I would expect significant symptom reduction within 3-6 months of consistent CPT participation. Full return to pre-MVA functioning is a reasonable long-term goal, though some residual hypervigilance and driving anxiety may persist and require ongoing management. A follow-up IME in 6 months is recommended to assess treatment response and updated functional capacity.'

Note: This IME summary is included in the clinical record with Alex's written consent (signed September 20, 2025). Alex expressed ambivalence about the IME process ('it felt like they were trying to prove I'm faking it') but acknowledged the examiner was professional and thorough. Clinician processed feelings about the IME in session 8.
COMPREHENSIVE INTAKE ASSESSMENT
Lakeshore Behavioral Health Center
Confidential Clinical Document
Client: Alex Rivera (they/them) | DOB: 1997-11-03 | Age: 28
Clinician: Dr. Samira Okonkwo, PsyD
Date of Assessment: July 18, 2025
Referral Source: Self-referred via PCP recommendation (Dr. Elena Vasquez, MD)
IDENTIFYING INFORMATION

Alex Rivera is a 28-year-old non-binary individual (they/them pronouns) who presents as a self-referred outpatient seeking treatment for symptoms following a motor vehicle accident (MVA) approximately 8 months prior to intake. Alex identifies as Mexican-American, is a cisgender non-binary person, and speaks English as their primary language with conversational Spanish. They reside in a two-bedroom apartment with their partner of three years, Jordan Mitchell (he/him), in the Lakeshore neighborhood. Alex is employed full-time as a senior graphic designer at Meridian Creative Agency, though they report reduced work capacity since the MVA. They hold a Bachelor of Fine Arts from the University of Illinois at Chicago (2019). Alex carries private insurance through their employer (Blue Cross Blue Shield PPO). Emergency contact: Jordan Mitchell, partner, (312) 555-0147.
PRESENTING PROBLEM

Alex reports the primary reason for seeking treatment is 'I can't stop reliving the accident and I feel like I'm losing myself.' On November 15, 2024, Alex was driving home from a work event at approximately 9:45 PM on a rain-slicked highway when a commercial truck crossed the median and struck their vehicle head-on at approximately 55 mph. Alex was trapped in the vehicle for approximately 40 minutes before emergency extraction. They sustained a cervical spine sprain (C4-C6), multiple contusions, a left wrist fracture (since healed), and a mild traumatic brain injury. They were hospitalized for 5 days at Northwestern Memorial Hospital.

Since the MVA, Alex reports the following symptoms with onset within 2 weeks post-accident:
- Intrusive memories of the accident occurring 4-6 times daily, often triggered by traffic sounds, rain, headlights at night, or the smell of gasoline
- Recurrent nightmares about the accident (3-5 nights per week), waking in a panic with sweating and tachycardia
- Dissociative flashback episodes (2-3 per week) lasting 30 seconds to several minutes, particularly when riding in vehicles
- Intense psychological distress when exposed to reminders (highways, news about accidents, the intersection near the accident site)
- Marked physiological reactivity (heart racing, sweating, nausea, trembling) to accident cues
- Persistent avoidance of driving (has not driven since the accident; relies on partner, rideshare, or public transit)
- Avoidance of conversations about the accident, declining to discuss details even with partner or close friends
- Avoidance of the highway where the accident occurred and surrounding roads
- Persistent negative beliefs: 'The world is completely dangerous,' 'I can never be safe,' 'It was my fault for driving in the rain'
- Persistent distorted cognitions about the cause of the accident, including self-blame despite the truck driver being found at fault
- Markedly diminished interest in previously enjoyed activities (drawing, cycling, social gatherings)
- Feelings of detachment and estrangement from others, including partner and close friends
- Persistent inability to experience positive emotions; reports feeling 'numb' or 'flat' most of the time
- Irritability and anger outbursts (2-3 times per week), often directed at partner over minor issues
- Hypervigilance in all settings but especially in vehicles and near roadways
- Exaggerated startle response to loud or sudden noises
- Concentration difficulties impacting work performance; reports making errors on design projects
- Sleep disturbance: initial insomnia (takes 60-90 minutes to fall asleep), frequent awakenings (3-4 per night), early morning awakening; total sleep approximately 4-5 hours per night despite trazodone
PSYCHIATRIC HISTORY

Alex reports one prior episode of Major Depressive Disorder at age 22 (2019-2020), coinciding with college graduation and transition to full-time employment. Symptoms included depressed mood, anhedonia, hypersomnia, poor concentration, and passive suicidal ideation without plan or intent. They engaged in 14 sessions of supportive psychotherapy with a licensed clinical social worker (LCSW) at their university counseling center, followed by 6 months of sertraline 50mg prescribed by their PCP. The depressive episode resolved, and Alex discontinued sertraline after 8 months with PCP guidance, remaining stable until the MVA.

No prior history of PTSD, anxiety disorders, psychotic symptoms, manic or hypomanic episodes, eating disorders, or personality disorder features prior to the MVA. No prior psychiatric hospitalizations. No history of self-harm behavior.
MEDICAL HISTORY

Current medical conditions:
- Chronic cervical spine pain secondary to MVA (C4-C6 sprain), managed with physical therapy (2x/week) and as-needed naproxen 500mg
- Resolved left wrist fracture (cast removed February 2025, full range of motion restored)
- Mild TBI (post-concussive symptoms resolved by 3 months post-MVA per neurology follow-up)
- Chronic tension headaches (2-3 per week since MVA), managed with acetaminophen
- No history of seizures, head injuries prior to MVA, or neurological conditions

Current medications:
- Sertraline 100mg daily (restarted January 2025 by PCP for depression/anxiety)
- Trazodone 50mg at bedtime (prescribed February 2025 for insomnia)
- Naproxen 500mg as needed for neck pain
- Acetaminophen 500mg as needed for headaches
- Melatonin 5mg at bedtime (OTC, self-initiated)

Allergies: No known drug allergies. Seasonal allergies managed with loratadine.
Last physical exam: March 2025 — within normal limits aside from noted conditions.
SUBSTANCE USE ASSESSMENT

Alcohol: Alex reports increased alcohol consumption since the MVA. Pre-MVA, they drank socially (1-2 drinks per weekend). Currently consuming 3-4 alcoholic beverages per night (typically craft beer or wine), primarily to 'quiet my mind' and facilitate sleep onset. They acknowledge this represents a significant increase and express some concern. On weekends, consumption may increase to 5-6 drinks. Denies morning drinking, withdrawal symptoms, or blackouts, though reports occasional memory gaps for evening events. AUDIT score at intake: 18 (hazardous/harmful use zone).

Cannabis: Uses cannabis edibles (THC gummies, 10-15mg) approximately 2 times per week, typically on evenings when anxiety is particularly acute. Reports this was occasional (1-2 times per month) pre-MVA. Denies daily use or functional impairment from cannabis.
Caffeine: 3-4 cups of coffee daily; notes increased intake to combat fatigue from poor sleep.
Nicotine: Denies current or past tobacco/nicotine use.
Other substances: Denies use of illicit drugs, misuse of prescription medications, or history of substance use disorders. No prior substance use treatment.
FAMILY HISTORY

Mother, Maria Rivera (age 56): History of Major Depressive Disorder, treated with fluoxetine since age 40. Currently stable. Described as 'emotionally supportive but sometimes overbearing.' Close relationship.

Father, Carlos Rivera (age 59): History of Alcohol Use Disorder; achieved sobriety at age 45 (14 years ago) through AA. Alex describes a complicated relationship — 'He's been sober for a long time but I still carry the memories of his drinking.' Father was verbally aggressive when drinking but never physically abusive to Alex. Relationship improved since sobriety.
Sibling, Isabella Rivera (age 25): No known psychiatric history. Works as an elementary school teacher. Close relationship; primary social support alongside partner.
Maternal grandmother: History of 'nervios' (likely anxiety disorder) — never formally treated. Deceased at 78 (cardiovascular disease).
Paternal grandfather: History of alcohol dependence. Deceased at 62 (liver disease).
No family history of completed suicide, bipolar disorder, or psychotic disorders.
SOCIAL AND DEVELOPMENTAL HISTORY

Alex was born and raised in the Pilsen neighborhood of Chicago in a working-class Mexican-American family. They describe a childhood that was 'mostly good but with some hard parts.' Alex witnessed domestic verbal altercations between parents during father's active drinking period (ages 5-14), which they describe as frightening. They recall hiding in their room with younger sister during arguments, feeling responsible for protecting Isabella. Father entered AA when Alex was 14, and family dynamics improved significantly.

Alex was a high-achieving student, particularly in visual arts. They describe some peer difficulties in middle school related to gender identity exploration — 'I didn't have the words for it then, but I always felt different.' Alex came out as non-binary at age 23 and reports generally positive reception from family and social circle, though notes some extended family members 'don't really get it.'

Relationship: In a committed relationship with Jordan Mitchell for 3 years. Alex describes the relationship as 'the best thing in my life' but acknowledges significant strain since the MVA. They report emotional withdrawal, decreased sexual intimacy, increased irritability directed at Jordan, and guilt about 'being a burden.' Jordan has expressed concern and frustration but remains supportive. No history of relationship violence.

Social functioning: Pre-MVA, Alex maintained a close friend group (4-5 individuals), participated in a cycling club, attended community art events, and was active on social media sharing their artwork. Since the MVA, they have withdrawn from nearly all social activities, declined invitations, and stopped posting artwork. Reports feeling 'disconnected from everyone' and 'like nobody understands what I'm going through.'

Occupational functioning: Alex has been employed at Meridian Creative Agency for 4 years with strong performance reviews. Since the MVA, they report difficulty meeting deadlines, concentration errors in design work, reduced creative output, and conflict avoidance with colleagues. Supervisor granted informal accommodations (flexible deadlines, option to work from home 3 days/week). Alex expresses fear of job loss and identity erosion — 'Design is who I am, and I can't even do it right anymore.'
TRAUMA HISTORY

Index Trauma (Criterion A): Motor vehicle accident on November 15, 2024, as described above. Alex experienced actual threatened death and serious injury. They report believing they were going to die while trapped in the vehicle.

Childhood Adverse Experiences:
- Witnessed domestic verbal altercations between parents (ages 5-14) during father's active alcohol use. While not directly physically abused, Alex describes fear, hypervigilance at home, and a sense of unpredictability. They report this experience created a template of 'the world can become unsafe at any moment.'
- Experienced peer bullying in middle school (ages 11-13) related to perceived gender nonconformity. Reports being called slurs and excluded from social groups. Did not disclose to parents at the time.
- No history of sexual abuse, physical abuse directed at Alex, or other Criterion A traumas prior to the MVA.

Prior trauma responses: Alex reports that the childhood experiences 'made me anxious but I pushed through.' They did not develop PTSD symptoms from childhood events but acknowledge a longstanding pattern of hypervigilance and difficulty trusting that 'things will be okay.' The MVA appears to have activated and intensified these preexisting vulnerability patterns.
MENTAL STATUS EXAMINATION
Appearance: Well-groomed individual presenting in casual attire appropriate for setting. Appears stated age. Notable dark circles under eyes consistent with reported sleep deprivation.

Behavior: Cooperative and engaged throughout interview. Fidgeted with hands and shifted position frequently. Made intermittent eye contact. Became visibly distressed (tearful, voice trembling, increased fidgeting) when discussing MVA details and trauma history. Requested a brief break during trauma history discussion.
Speech: Normal rate and volume, though became halting and quieter when discussing traumatic material. No pressure of speech.
Mood: 'Exhausted and on edge' (client's words).

Affect: Constricted range. Predominantly anxious and dysphoric. Affect congruent with stated mood. Tearful at times. Brief brightening when discussing artwork and partner (pre-MVA). Flat affect when discussing current daily functioning.
Thought Process: Linear and goal-directed. No loosening of associations, tangentiality, or circumstantiality. Some cognitive rigidity noted around self-blame cognitions related to the MVA.

Thought Content: Preoccupied with MVA and safety concerns. Endorses passive suicidal ideation: 'Sometimes I think everyone would be better off without me, or I wish I hadn't survived the accident.' Denies active suicidal ideation, plan, intent, or access to means. Denies homicidal ideation. No delusions or paranoid ideation.

Perceptions: Endorses dissociative flashbacks (visual and somatic re-experiencing of the MVA). Denies auditory or visual hallucinations outside of flashback context. No derealization or depersonalization outside of trauma re-experiencing episodes.

Cognition: Oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Attention mildly impaired — required repetition of two questions during interview. Immediate and delayed recall grossly intact on informal testing (3/3 items immediate, 2/3 after 5 minutes). Fund of knowledge appropriate. Insight: Fair — recognizes symptoms as problematic and connected to MVA but demonstrates cognitive distortions (self-blame, overgeneralization of danger). Judgment: Fair — seeking treatment appropriately, maintaining employment, but engaging in increased alcohol use as coping strategy.
RISK ASSESSMENT

Suicidal Ideation: Present — passive. Alex endorses intermittent thoughts of 'not wanting to be here' and wishing they had not survived the MVA. Frequency: 2-3 times per week. Duration: Minutes. Denies plan, intent, means access, or preparatory behavior. C-SSRS Severity Rating at intake: 2 (Non-specific active suicidal thoughts).

Risk Factors: Current psychiatric symptoms (PTSD, MDD), passive suicidal ideation, increased alcohol use, chronic pain, sleep deprivation, social withdrawal, family history of depression and AUD, childhood adversity, identity-related stressors.

Protective Factors: Supportive partner, family connections (mother, sister), employment, future-oriented goals (career advancement, artwork), engagement in treatment, no prior suicide attempts, no access to firearms, willingness to safety plan.

Safety Plan Developed: Yes. (1) Warning signs: feeling trapped, intense accident memories, alcohol craving. (2) Internal coping: deep breathing, grounding techniques, drawing. (3) Social contacts: Jordan, Isabella, friend Marco. (4) Professional contacts: crisis line 988, therapist on-call. (5) Means restriction: agreed to limit alcohol in home, partner to monitor. (6) Reason for living: relationship with Jordan, career goals, artwork.
Overall Risk Level: MODERATE. Passive SI without plan/intent, multiple protective factors, safety plan in place. Outpatient level of care appropriate with close monitoring.
DIAGNOSTIC IMPRESSIONS

Axis I (DSM-5-TR):
1. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder (F43.10) — meets full criteria. Criterion A: MVA with threatened death/serious injury. Criteria B-E: intrusion, avoidance, negative cognitions/mood, arousal/reactivity symptoms present >1 month with significant distress and functional impairment. Specifier: With dissociative symptoms (flashbacks with altered awareness).
2. Major Depressive Disorder, Single Episode, Moderate (F32.1) — depressed mood, anhedonia, insomnia, fatigue, concentration difficulty, feelings of worthlessness, passive SI. PHQ-9: 17 (moderately severe).
3. Generalized Anxiety Disorder (F41.1) — excessive worry extending beyond trauma-related content to occupational, relational, and health domains. GAD-7: 15 (severe).
4. Rule Out Alcohol Use Disorder, Mild (F10.10) — AUDIT: 18. Increased consumption, using to cope, some concern. Monitoring recommended.

Differential diagnoses considered and ruled out: Adjustment Disorder (symptoms exceed adjustment in severity and meet PTSD criteria), Acute Stress Disorder (onset >1 month ago), Panic Disorder (panic symptoms occur in trauma-related contexts only), Persistent Complex Bereavement (no bereavement trigger).
FUNCTIONAL ASSESSMENT
WHO Disability Assessment Schedule 2.0 (WHODAS) Score: 38/48 (significant disability).

Domains of Impairment:
- Cognition: Moderate impairment. Difficulty concentrating on work tasks, forgetfulness in daily activities, slowed processing.
- Mobility: Mild-moderate impairment. Avoids driving entirely. Limited in walking near busy roads due to hypervigilance. Chronic neck pain limits some physical activities.
- Self-care: Mild impairment. Occasional neglect of grooming on high-symptom days. Irregular meal patterns (often skips lunch, overeats at night).
- Getting Along: Moderate-severe impairment. Withdrawal from partner, friends, and colleagues. Difficulty maintaining intimacy. Irritability straining relationships.
- Life Activities: Moderate impairment. Work performance declined. Household task completion reduced. Unable to engage in hobbies (cycling, art) at pre-MVA levels.
- Participation: Severe impairment. Near-complete withdrawal from social, community, and recreational activities. Avoidance of public spaces associated with driving/traffic.
Global Assessment of Functioning (estimated): 48 — serious symptoms with serious impairment in social and occupational functioning.

TREATMENT RECOMMENDATIONS:
1. Individual psychotherapy: Cognitive Processing Therapy (CPT) recommended as first-line evidence-based treatment for PTSD, with adaptations for comorbid MDD.
2. Frequency: Weekly 60-minute sessions.
3. Medication management: Continue sertraline 100mg and trazodone 50mg; coordinate with PCP regarding potential dose optimization.
4. Substance use monitoring: Track alcohol use weekly; psychoeducation on alcohol-trauma interaction; motivational interviewing as needed.
5. Safety monitoring: Review safety plan each session; C-SSRS screening at each visit.
6. Coordination of care: PCP (Dr. Vasquez), physical therapist, employer (accommodations).
7. Consider couples/partner session if relational distress persists.

Clinician Signature: Dr. Samira Okonkwo, PsyD
License: PsyD, IL License #071-012345
Date: July 18, 2025

Alex completed Challenging Questions for stuck point #1. Reports 'seeing some holes in my thinking but the guilt is still there.' 2 nightmares (decrease). Alcohol 2-3 nightly. PHQ-9 administered: 15 (down from 17). PCL-5: 58 (down from 64).

Demonstrating improved ability to challenge self-blame cognitively. 'The police report says the truck driver was at fault. I keep overriding that with my feelings.' Good identification of thinking patterns. Engaged with safety module — connected childhood hypervigilance to current safety beliefs.
Complete Patterns of Problematic Thinking worksheet for a current stuck point. Read CPT module on Safety. Journal one experience where you noticed overgeneralization of danger.

Measurable symptom improvement on PCL-5 and PHQ-9 at 4-week mark. Client is an excellent CPT candidate — intellectually engaged, emotionally present, and homework-compliant. Connection between childhood DV and current safety schema is emerging and will be important to explore. First assessment timepoint shows encouraging trajectory.
CPT Safety module continued; challenge safety stuck point

Alex reports 2 nightmares (stable). Identified 3 overgeneralization instances this week. Reports 'catching myself' in stuck point thinking. Alcohol 2 drinks nightly (continued gradual decrease). Jordan commented Alex seems 'a little more present.'

Continued CPT Safety module. Used Challenging Beliefs Worksheet (CBW) for stuck point #2: 'The world is completely dangerous.' Examined evidence — identified confirmation bias (attending only to threat cues). Explored how childhood experiences with father's drinking created early safety schema. Introduced concept of shattered assumptions.

Strong cognitive engagement. Able to generate balanced belief: 'The world has dangers but is not entirely dangerous; I survived and am building safety.' Rating of original belief decreased from 95% to 70%. Emotional — 'I think my dad's drinking taught me to always expect the worst.'
Complete CBW for one additional safety-related stuck point. Practice balanced safety thought daily. Notice moments of relative safety and log them.

Excellent progress on safety schema. Childhood connection is deepening understanding — this is not just MVA-related PTSD but activation of longstanding vulnerability. Alcohol continuing to decrease without direct intervention — likely secondary to symptom improvement. Partner noticing positive changes. Continue CPT trajectory.
Transition to Trust module; continue safety work as needed

Alex had a difficult week — work deadline triggered concentration difficulties and self-criticism. 3 nightmares (slight increase). Reports feeling 'frustrated with how slow this is.' Alcohol stable at 2 drinks nightly. No SI.

Validated frustration while normalizing non-linear recovery. Transitioned to CPT Trust module. Explored stuck point: 'I can't trust anyone to keep me safe, including myself.' Used CBW. Connected to childhood: 'I couldn't trust my dad when he was drinking. Now I can't trust other drivers, can't trust my own decisions.' Addressed work-related self-criticism as extension of trust-in-self deficit.

Initially frustrated and somewhat shut down. Opened up as trust theme resonated deeply. Made powerful connection between father and current trust difficulties unprompted. 'I've been waiting for the other shoe to drop my whole life. The accident was the shoe.' Work self-criticism decreased as Alex connected it to broader trust schema rather than actual incompetence.
Complete CBW for trust-in-self stuck point ('I can't trust my own judgment'). Write a letter to 14-year-old self about what they learned about trust from father's recovery.

Productive session despite initial frustration. The childhood DV connection is proving therapeutically rich — it contextualizes the PTSD within a longer developmental narrative. Trust schema work is addressing both trauma-specific and characterological patterns. Work functioning concerns are valid but appear cognitively mediated. Consider discussing formal workplace accommodations.
Continue Trust module; transition to Power/Control

SIGNIFICANT SETBACK. Alex had a severe re-experiencing episode while a passenger in Jordan's car — a truck passed closely and Alex had a full dissociative flashback lasting ~3 minutes. Subsequent panic attack. Reported drinking 5 drinks that evening and 4-5 drinks nightly since. Nightmares returned to 5/week. Endorsed passive SI: 'What's the point if I'm just going to keep reliving this.' Reports feeling 'back at square one' and questioning whether therapy is working.

Crisis stabilization and grounding (5-4-3-2-1 sensory technique used in session when Alex became highly activated). Validated distress while challenging 'back to square one' narrative with evidence of progress (reviewed symptom trajectory). Addressed increased alcohol use directly — psychoeducation on alcohol's exacerbation of PTSD symptoms, particularly nightmares and hyperarousal. Motivational interviewing: explored ambivalence about drinking. Updated safety plan. Did NOT continue CPT protocol — stabilization priority.

Highly distressed at session start. Grounding was effective — returned to window of tolerance within 10 minutes. Resistant initially to reframing progress ('You don't understand how terrifying it was'). Gradually acknowledged that pre-treatment, the flashback would have been worse and lasted longer. Receptive to alcohol psychoeducation — 'I know the drinking is making it worse but it's the only thing that shuts my brain off.' Agreed to attempt reducing to 2 drinks maximum.

Use grounding techniques immediately when triggered. Limit alcohol to 2 drinks maximum. Complete a 'before and after therapy' comparison to counter 'back to square one' thinking. Call therapist if SI increases.
N/A - crisis session, prior homework interrupted

Setback is concerning but not unexpected in CPT trajectory. The in-vivo exposure to the truck trigger was intense and overwhelmed current coping capacity. Alcohol increase is the primary safety concern — if sustained, may need to discuss AUD diagnosis and possible referral for substance-specific treatment. SI returned but remains passive. Alliance took a hit — Alex is questioning treatment efficacy. Critical to validate and stabilize next session before resuming CPT. Will consult supervisor.
Moderate-High - passive SI returned, alcohol increase, flashback severity
Stabilization continued; alcohol monitoring; determine readiness to resume CPT

Alex presents calmer than last week. Reports using grounding 4 times since last session, 'and it actually helped twice.' Alcohol reduced to 3 drinks nightly (down from 5 but above pre-setback). Nightmares 4/week. Completed 'before/after' comparison. SI: 1 fleeting thought, used safety plan. PHQ-9: 18 (increase). PCL-5: 60 (increase from 58). AUDIT: 16. GAD-7: 16.

Reviewed 'before/after' comparison — Alex acknowledged gains: 'I guess I'm not completely back to square one. I know what stuck points are now and I caught myself in one.' Processed setback experience using CPT framework — identified catastrophizing ('one bad episode means I'll never get better') and emotional reasoning. Resumed CPT Power/Control module gently. Introduced controlled breathing as pre-sleep routine to address alcohol-as-sleep-aid pattern.

More regulated than session 7. The before/after exercise was effective — Alex could articulate specific skills gained. Engaged cautiously with Power/Control module. 'I used to feel so in control of my life. The accident took that away.' Connected to childhood feeling of powerlessness during father's drinking episodes. Alliance rebuilding — 'I'm sorry I was so shut down last week. I know you're trying to help.'
Continue grounding practice. Implement pre-sleep breathing routine (replace one drink with breathing exercise). CBW for Power/Control stuck point: 'I have no control over what happens to me.'
Partial - completed before/after but not grounding log consistently

Assessment scores show expected dip at session 8 — consistent with setback. However, client's in-session presentation is actually improved from session 7, suggesting recovery is underway. Alcohol remains elevated but trending down. The before/after comparison was a valuable intervention for combating demoralization. Childhood powerlessness theme is layering productively into CPT Power/Control module. Supervisor consultation completed — agree to continue outpatient with enhanced monitoring. If alcohol does not decrease by session 10, will make formal referral.
Moderate - SI fleeting, alcohol still elevated, but improving
Continue Power/Control; monitor alcohol closely; resume CPT progression

Alex reports 'best week in a while.' Grounding used successfully 3 times. Pre-sleep breathing routine implemented 5/7 nights — 'fell asleep faster on those nights.' Alcohol 2 drinks nightly. Nightmares 3/week (improving). Completed CBW for Power/Control. Reports taking the bus alone for the first time since the MVA. No SI.

Celebrated bus achievement as significant behavioral evidence against powerlessness belief. Reviewed Power/Control CBW — Alex generated strong alternative: 'I don't control everything but I have more control than I think. I chose to get on that bus.' Introduced CPT Esteem module. Explored stuck point: 'I am broken and will never be the person I was.' Socratic questioning revealed this stuck point connects to identity as artist/designer — pre-MVA self as idealized, post-MVA self as diminished.

Markedly improved affect — first genuine smile observed in session. Pride in bus accomplishment evident. Engaged deeply with Esteem work. Tearful but not overwhelmed: 'Maybe I'm not the same person, but maybe that's not all bad. I've learned things about myself I didn't know.' Beginning to integrate trauma experience rather than solely viewing it as destructive.
CBW for esteem stuck point. Do one creative activity this week (drawing, sketching — anything). Write about what you've learned about yourself since the accident that is positive.

Significant shift this session. The bus ride represents genuine behavioral change and natural exposure. Alcohol back to manageable level. Sleep improving with behavioral intervention. The esteem work is opening a path from 'broken' to 'changed but growing.' Childhood themes continue to enrich the formulation — the idealized pre-trauma self may partly reflect a coping strategy from childhood (being 'perfect' to manage chaotic home environment). Will explore this sensitively.
Continue Esteem module; explore identity integration

Alex completed a drawing for the first time since the MVA — a charcoal piece depicting 'the space between the crash and now.' Brought it to session. Reports feeling 'something unlock.' Alcohol 1-2 drinks nightly. Nightmares 2/week. Took bus 3 times this week. Jordan reports Alex 'seems more like themselves.' No SI.

Explored the artwork therapeutically — Alex described it as 'showing that the space between the crash and now isn't empty, it's full of all this work.' Used art as bridge to Esteem module completion. Introduced Intimacy module. Explored relational withdrawal and reconnection with Jordan. Discussed how PTSD symptoms impacted intimacy (emotional and physical). Connected to childhood pattern of withdrawal when environment felt unsafe.

Emotionally present and engaged throughout. The artwork represented a breakthrough — creative identity reconnecting. 'I'm not the designer I was before the accident, but maybe I'm becoming something different.' Intimacy discussion brought some vulnerability — acknowledged avoiding Jordan physically out of numbness and shame. 'I feel like damaged goods sometimes.' Identified this as stuck point.
CBW for intimacy stuck point ('I am damaged goods'). Plan one intentional connection activity with Jordan this week. Continue drawing/creative work.

The artwork is clinically significant — it represents integration of the trauma narrative into Alex's core identity as a creative person. This is exactly the kind of meaning-making that distinguishes recovery from mere symptom reduction. Intimacy work is timely — the relationship is a key protective factor and Alex is now emotionally regulated enough to engage with relational vulnerability. Consider recommending a couples session in the future.
Continue Intimacy module; consider partner session

Alex and Jordan had a 'real conversation' this week about how the accident affected both of them. Jordan cried, Alex held them. 'First time I didn't feel numb.' Alcohol 1 drink most nights, 0 on 2 nights. Nightmares 1-2/week. Took bus daily. Considering test-driving in a parking lot with Jordan. Reports new insight: 'I think I've been protecting people from my pain the way I used to protect Isabella from Dad's yelling.'

Processed the partner conversation and its emotional significance. Explored the powerful childhood insight that Alex generated independently — the protective role they assumed with Isabella mapped onto current withdrawal pattern ('if I keep everyone away, they won't be hurt by my pain'). This represents high-level CPT integration — connecting childhood schema to post-trauma behavior without clinician prompting. Completed Intimacy module. Began final integration and relapse prevention planning.

Deeply insightful session. Alex is demonstrating genuine cognitive and emotional integration of trauma material. The spontaneous childhood connection shows internalization of CPT skills. Affect is fuller — sadness, tenderness, hope all present. Humor returned for the first time: joked about 'my brain doing its CPT homework without me.' Therapeutic relationship at strongest point.
Write revised Impact Statement (comparing to original). Begin relapse prevention plan: list warning signs, coping strategies, support contacts. Drive in parking lot if comfortable (no pressure).

This session represents the culmination of excellent CPT engagement. The independent childhood insight is remarkable and suggests deep integration. The relational reconnection with Jordan is a key prognostic indicator. Alcohol is now within non-hazardous range without direct substance-focused intervention — confirms it was secondary to PTSD. Driving consideration is a major avoidance-reduction milestone. Plan to complete treatment in session 12 with relapse prevention, revised impact statement, and discharge planning.
Low - no SI, strong protective factors active
Revised impact statement; relapse prevention; discharge planning

Final CPT session. Alex read revised impact statement — markedly different from original. 'The accident didn't break me. It broke open something that was already cracked from childhood, and now I'm putting it back together differently.' Drove in parking lot with Jordan twice. Nightmares 1/week. Alcohol 0-1 drink nightly. PCL-5: 34 (significant decrease). PHQ-9: 9 (mild). GAD-7: 10 (moderate). AUDIT: 8 (below hazardous threshold). No SI for 4 weeks.

Processed revised impact statement — compared with original to highlight growth. Reviewed all stuck points and current belief ratings. Completed relapse prevention plan collaboratively. Discussed warning signs (sleep disruption, avoidance increase, alcohol increase, withdrawal from Jordan). Reviewed CPT skills as ongoing tools. Discussed transition to maintenance phase: biweekly sessions for 2 months, then monthly. Addressed termination feelings. Provided referral list for couples therapy if desired. Coordinated with PCP re: medication review.

Poised and reflective. Read revised impact statement with steady voice and full affect — tears present but no dysregulation. 'I'm not done healing but I have the tools now.' Expressed gratitude for therapy while also acknowledging own effort. Discussed anxiety about ending weekly sessions — normalized and framed as part of trust-in-self work. 'I guess this is me trusting that I can handle it.'

Continue using CPT skills independently. Maintain relapse prevention plan. Schedule biweekly follow-up appointments. Continue gradual driving exposure. Consider couples therapy referral. Follow up with PCP on medication.
Full - revised impact statement and relapse prevention plan completed

Outstanding treatment response over 12 sessions. PCL-5 dropped from 64 to 34 (clinically significant, below diagnostic threshold). PHQ-9 from 17 to 9 (moderate to mild). AUDIT from 18 to 8 (resolved hazardous use). Alex has internalized CPT framework and independently connected childhood schemas to post-trauma functioning. The revised impact statement demonstrates genuine post-traumatic growth. Transitioning to maintenance phase. Key remaining goals: continued driving exposure, medication optimization, and monitoring for relapse. Supervision note: exemplary CPT case with childhood trauma complication that enriched rather than derailed treatment.
Transition to biweekly maintenance; continue exposure; PCP coordination

content.text: TREATMENT HISTORY & MEDICATION RECORD
Client: Alex Rivera (they/them) | DOB: 1997-11-03
Prepared for treatment coordination purposes
Confidential
PRIOR PSYCHOTHERAPY

Episode 1 (2019-2020): Supportive psychotherapy for Major Depressive Disorder
- Provider: Lisa Chen, LCSW, University of Illinois at Chicago Counseling Center
- Duration: 14 sessions over 7 months (September 2019 - March 2020)
- Modality: Individual, supportive/eclectic orientation
- Presenting concerns: Depressed mood, anhedonia, hypersomnia, poor concentration, passive suicidal ideation coinciding with college graduation and career transition
- Treatment focus: Adjustment to post-college life, identity development, family-of-origin issues (father's alcoholism, caretaking role), career anxiety
- Outcome: Symptoms remitted by session 10. Final PHQ-9: 4 (minimal). Client developed effective coping strategies including journaling, physical activity, and social connection. Maintained employment and built social network. Therapy termination was collaborative and planned.
- Transfer note: 'Alex is a thoughtful, insightful client who responded well to supportive psychotherapy. They demonstrated particular strength in reflective capacity and willingness to examine family dynamics. The depressive episode appeared situational, triggered by life transition, with underlying vulnerability related to childhood family instability. Recommend monitoring for future depressive episodes during high-stress periods, as well as attention to the caretaking pattern that emerged as a central dynamic.' — L. Chen, LCSW, March 2020

No therapy engagement between March 2020 and July 2025 (current treatment). Alex reports using self-directed coping strategies (exercise, creative expression, social support) to manage subclinical anxiety during this period. No crisis contacts or emergency visits.
MEDICATION HISTORY

Medication Trial 1: Sertraline (Zoloft)
- Initial prescription: September 2019, 50mg daily, by PCP (Dr. Robert Kim, MD)
- Indication: Major Depressive Disorder
- Response: Good response within 4-6 weeks. Side effects: mild nausea (first 2 weeks, resolved), mild sexual dysfunction (persistent but tolerable)
- Duration: 8 months (September 2019 - May 2020)
- Discontinuation: Tapered over 4 weeks with PCP guidance. No discontinuation syndrome. Stable after cessation.

Medication Trial 2: Sertraline (Zoloft) — restarted
- Prescription: January 15, 2025, 50mg daily, titrated to 100mg after 2 weeks, by PCP (Dr. Elena Vasquez, MD)
- Indication: PTSD with comorbid MDD and GAD, post-MVA
- Response: Partial response at 100mg. Modest improvement in baseline anxiety and mood after 6 weeks, but PTSD core symptoms (re-experiencing, avoidance, hyperarousal) minimally affected. Patient reports sertraline 'takes the edge off' but does not address nightmares, flashbacks, or avoidance.
- Side effects: Mild nausea (first week, resolved), sexual dysfunction (moderate — contributing to intimacy difficulties with partner), mild weight gain (5 lbs over 6 months)
- Current status: Continuing 100mg daily. PCP considering dose increase to 150mg or augmentation pending therapy progress assessment.

Medication Trial 3: Trazodone
- Prescription: February 10, 2025, 50mg at bedtime, by PCP (Dr. Elena Vasquez, MD)
- Indication: Insomnia secondary to PTSD
- Response: Modest improvement in sleep onset latency (reduced from 90 to 60 minutes). Minimal effect on nightmares or middle-of-night awakenings. Total sleep increased from 4 to 5 hours but remains insufficient.
- Side effects: Morning grogginess (first 2 weeks, improved), dry mouth
- Current status: Continuing 50mg at bedtime. PCP may increase to 100mg if sleep remains disrupted.

Medication Trial 4: Buspirone (BuSpar)
- Prescription: March 2025, 5mg twice daily, titrated to 10mg twice daily, by PCP
- Indication: Generalized Anxiety Disorder augmentation
- Response: Minimal response after 6 weeks at target dose. Patient reported 'I couldn't tell it was doing anything.'
- Side effects: Dizziness, headache
- Discontinuation: Tapered and discontinued May 2025 due to lack of efficacy and side effects.
- Note: PCP deferred further medication adjustments pending initiation of psychotherapy, noting that evidence-based trauma-focused therapy (CPT or PE) is first-line for PTSD and may reduce need for pharmacological augmentation.
PCP COORDINATION NOTES

Coordination Call: Dr. Elena Vasquez, MD (PCP) and Dr. Samira Okonkwo, PsyD
Date: July 22, 2025 (4 days post-intake)

Summary of Discussion:
- Reviewed intake assessment findings and diagnostic impressions
- Agreed on PTSD as primary diagnosis with comorbid MDD and GAD
- Discussed AUDIT score of 18 — PCP will monitor liver function (LFTs) at next visit, scheduled for August
- PCP will maintain current medication regimen (sertraline 100mg, trazodone 50mg) during initial phase of CPT to avoid confounding variables
- Agreed that if PTSD symptoms significantly improve with CPT, medication taper may be considered in 3-6 months
- If PTSD symptoms remain refractory to CPT, PCP will consider: (a) sertraline increase to 150-200mg, (b) prazosin 1-2mg at bedtime for nightmares, or (c) psychiatric referral for medication management
- PCP noted chronic neck pain is being managed with PT and naproxen; recent cervical MRI showed improvement in soft tissue inflammation
- Alex's next PCP appointment: August 15, 2025

Coordination Call: Dr. Elena Vasquez, MD and Dr. Samira Okonkwo, PsyD
Date: September 16, 2025 (post-setback, after session 8)

Summary of Discussion:
- Informed PCP of setback at session 7 and alcohol escalation to 5 drinks/night
- PCP reviewed August LFTs: mildly elevated GGT (68 U/L, normal <55) — consistent with increased alcohol use
- PCP will recheck LFTs in October and counsel Alex on alcohol reduction at next visit
- Discussed adding prazosin for persistent nightmares — PCP agreed to prescribe prazosin 1mg at bedtime if nightmares do not improve with CPT resumption by session 10
- PCP will maintain sertraline at 100mg; does not want to increase during active CPT unless indicated
- Discussed SI return — PCP aware of safety plan; will assess at next visit
- Alex's next PCP appointment: October 1, 2025

Coordination Call: Dr. Elena Vasquez, MD and Dr. Samira Okonkwo, PsyD
Date: October 14, 2025 (post-session 12, treatment completion)

Summary of Discussion:
- Reviewed treatment outcome data: PCL-5 64→34, PHQ-9 17→9, AUDIT 18→8
- PCP notes October LFTs normalized (GGT 42 U/L)
- Agreed to maintain sertraline 100mg for at least 6 additional months given MDD history and recent acute stressor
- Trazodone may taper to PRN basis given sleep improvement — PCP to discuss with Alex at next visit
- Prazosin was not started as nightmares improved sufficiently with CPT
- Discussed transition to biweekly maintenance therapy sessions
- PCP will continue monitoring mood, sleep, and alcohol use at routine visits
- Next PCP appointment: November 12, 2025
PHYSICAL THERAPY NOTES (SUMMARY)

Provider: Lakeshore Physical Therapy Associates — Sarah Kim, DPT
Dates: December 2024 - present (ongoing, 2x/week reduced to 1x/week as of September 2025)
Diagnosis: Cervical spine sprain (C4-C6) secondary to MVA

Progress: Alex has shown steady improvement in cervical range of motion and pain levels. Initial presentation: significant limitation in flexion, extension, and rotation with pain rating 7/10. Current: mild limitation in extension only, pain 3/10 most days. Neck strengthening exercises performed consistently. PT notes that Alex's pain tends to flare during high-stress weeks, consistent with known stress-pain relationship. PT recommended continued 1x/week sessions through December 2025 with reassessment for discharge.

Relevant behavioral observation from PT: 'Alex appears more engaged and communicative in recent sessions (September-October). Earlier in treatment, they were withdrawn and would sometimes dissociate during exercises — I learned to watch for glazed expression and offer grounding cues. This has not occurred since mid-September.'
TREATMENT PLAN UPDATE & CLINICAL CASE FORMULATION
Lakeshore Behavioral Health Center
Client: Alex Rivera (they/them) | DOB: 1997-11-03
Date of Report: March 14, 2026
Treating Clinician: Dr. Samira Okonkwo, PsyD
Clinical Supervisor: Dr. James Whitfield, PhD, ABPP
Classification: CONFIDENTIAL — Protected Health Information
1. CLINICAL CASE FORMULATION (BIOPSYCHOSOCIAL)

Client: Alex Rivera (they/them) | DOB: 1997-11-03 | Age: 28
Date of Treatment Plan Update: March 14, 2026
Treating Clinician: Dr. Samira Okonkwo, PsyD
Supervisor: Dr. James Whitfield, PhD, ABPP
Facility: Lakeshore Behavioral Health Center

Predisposing Factors:
Biological: Family history of Major Depressive Disorder (mother, maternal grandmother probable anxiety disorder) and Alcohol Use Disorder (father, paternal grandfather) indicates significant genetic vulnerability to mood, anxiety, and substance use disorders. Alex experienced a prior depressive episode at age 22 that responded to sertraline and supportive therapy, confirming biological predisposition to affective dysregulation under stress. Neurobiologically, the combination of genetic loading and childhood adversity likely resulted in sensitized stress-response systems (HPA axis, amygdala reactivity) that created vulnerability to PTSD following a Criterion A traumatic event.

Psychological: Alex witnessed domestic verbal altercations between parents during father's active alcohol use (ages 5-14), creating early schemas of danger, unpredictability, and the need for hypervigilance. They adopted a caretaking/protective role with younger sister Isabella, developing a pattern of suppressing their own needs to manage environmental chaos. This caretaking pattern extended into a perfectionist, self-reliant coping style — excelling academically and professionally while avoiding acknowledgment of vulnerability. The childhood experience created cognitive templates (identified through CPT as core stuck points) including 'The world can become unsafe at any moment,' 'I must be in control to be safe,' and 'If I am perfect/strong, bad things won't happen.' Additionally, peer bullying during middle school related to gender nonconformity (ages 11-13) reinforced themes of vulnerability, difference, and the need to manage others' perceptions.

Social/Cultural: Alex identifies as Mexican-American and non-binary (they/them), navigating intersecting cultural identities. Their Mexican-American family heritage includes cultural values of familismo (family loyalty and closeness), which functions as both a strength (strong family bonds) and a potential stressor (family expectations, extended family's limited understanding of non-binary identity). Alex came out as non-binary at age 23 with generally positive reception but reports some extended family members 'don't really get it,' creating a background stressor related to authentic identity expression. The concept of 'nervios' in the maternal family may reflect culturally mediated anxiety experiences that normalized anxious distress without pathologizing it. Alex's professional identity as a graphic designer is deeply intertwined with their sense of self-worth, making the post-MVA creative impairment particularly threatening to identity cohesion.

Precipitating Factors:
The index trauma — a high-severity motor vehicle accident on November 15, 2024 — directly precipitated PTSD. The accident involved head-on collision with a commercial truck at approximately 55 mph, 40-minute entrapment, threatened death, and serious physical injury (cervical spine sprain, wrist fracture, mild TBI). The MVA activated and amplified preexisting vulnerability schemas from childhood. Specifically, the experience of being trapped, helpless, and in danger mirrored the childhood experience of hiding in their room during parental altercations — both involving powerlessness in the face of threat. Alex's self-blame cognition ('It was my fault for driving in the rain') parallels the childhood cognitive pattern of assuming responsibility for uncontrollable events (feeling responsible for protecting Isabella from father's behavior). The comorbid MDD represents reactivation of depressive vulnerability (prior episode at 22) under the sustained stress of PTSD, loss of functional capacity, and identity disruption. GAD reflects generalization of trauma-related hyperarousal and worry beyond accident-specific stimuli to occupational, relational, and health domains.

Perpetuating Factors:
- Cognitive: Rigid stuck points maintained PTSD symptom cycle, particularly self-blame, overgeneralization of danger, and shattered assumptions about safety, trust, and self-competence (addressed through CPT)
- Behavioral: Extensive avoidance (driving, social activities, emotional intimacy) prevented natural extinction of fear responses and corrective learning experiences
- Substance: Increased alcohol use (3-4 drinks nightly, peaking at 5 during setback) functioned as avoidance-based coping that exacerbated sleep disruption, emotional numbing, and depressive symptoms while providing short-term anxiolytic relief
- Physiological: Chronic sleep deprivation (4-5 hours/night) maintained hyperarousal, impaired emotional regulation, and reduced cognitive capacity for therapy engagement and daily functioning
- Medical: Chronic neck pain served as somatic trauma reminder and contributed to functional limitation, stress, and pain-sleep-mood cycle
- Relational: Emotional withdrawal from partner Jordan created isolation that reinforced depressive cognitions ('I am a burden') and reduced access to social support
- Occupational: Reduced work performance threatened identity and self-worth, reinforcing 'I am broken' stuck point

Protective Factors:
- Supportive and committed partner (Jordan Mitchell) who has remained engaged despite significant relationship strain
- Strong family connections (mother Maria, sister Isabella) providing cultural and emotional support
- Pre-morbid high functioning with strong educational and professional achievement
- Creative talent and artistic identity that became a vehicle for post-traumatic integration (charcoal drawing in session 10)
- Intellectual capacity for CPT — demonstrated excellent cognitive engagement with stuck point identification and challenging
- Young age (28) with full career ahead and neuroplasticity for recovery
- Willingness to engage in treatment and homework compliance (rated full completion in 8 of 12 sessions)
- No prior suicide attempts, no access to firearms, safety plan in place and utilized
- Employment maintained throughout treatment with supportive employer accommodations
2. DIAGNOSTIC UPDATE

The following diagnostic review is based on 12 sessions of clinical observation, standardized assessment data at four timepoints (intake, session 4, session 8, session 12), clinician judgment, and collateral information.

1. Posttraumatic Stress Disorder (F43.10) — MAINTAINED, severity reduced from Severe to Mild-Moderate.
Clinical Reasoning: Alex continues to meet diagnostic criteria for PTSD, though with significant symptom reduction. PCL-5 score decreased from 64 (intake) to 34 (session 12), a 30-point reduction representing clinically significant change (RCI threshold: 10-12 points). Current score of 34 is at the PCL-5 diagnostic threshold (31-33), indicating the client is in the borderline range. Cluster analysis shows: Cluster B (intrusion) reduced from 18 to 8 — nightmares reduced from 5/week to 1/week, flashbacks resolved, intrusive memories substantially decreased. Cluster C (avoidance) reduced from 8 to 4 — Alex has begun independent bus use and is considering driving. Cluster D (cognition/mood) reduced from 22 to 12 — core stuck points substantially modified, positive emotions returning, engagement with others improved. Cluster E (arousal) reduced from 16 to 10 — sleep improved, irritability decreased, startle response attenuated. Residual symptoms: occasional nightmares, mild hypervigilance in vehicles, residual cognitive distortions about complete safety. Dissociative specifier: no longer applicable — flashback episodes resolved since session 9.

2. Major Depressive Disorder, Single Episode — MAINTAINED, severity reduced from Moderate to Mild.
Clinical Reasoning: PHQ-9 decreased from 17 (moderately severe) to 9 (mild). Alex no longer meets criteria for moderate MDD. Current symptoms: residual sleep disturbance (improving), mild fatigue, intermittent concentration difficulty, mild anhedonia. Notable improvements: suicidal ideation resolved (SI-free for 4 weeks per C-SSRS), worthlessness/guilt substantially reduced, interest in activities returning (resumed drawing, social engagement). Alex demonstrates future-oriented thinking and appropriate affect range. Recommendation: maintain diagnosis at mild level during maintenance phase; consider remission designation if PHQ-9 remains below 5 for 8 consecutive weeks.

3. Generalized Anxiety Disorder (F41.1) — MAINTAINED, severity reduced from Severe to Moderate.
Clinical Reasoning: GAD-7 decreased from 15 (severe) to 10 (moderate). Alex reports reduced worry frequency and intensity across domains, particularly safety-related worry. Residual anxiety: worry about driving exposure, long-term recovery trajectory, and occupational performance. Somatic anxiety (muscle tension, restlessness) has improved but remains present. The boundary between PTSD-related hyperarousal and generalized anxiety remains somewhat blurred — some residual anxiety may be better accounted for by PTSD Cluster E symptoms. Continued monitoring will clarify diagnostic boundaries as PTSD continues to improve.

4. Alcohol Use Disorder — RULE-OUT RESOLVED.
Clinical Reasoning: AUDIT score decreased from 18 (harmful use) to 8 (low risk/at threshold). At intake, Alex's alcohol use pattern (3-4 drinks nightly, use to cope with distress, increasing tolerance) raised concern for mild AUD. However, the alcohol use pattern tracked directly with PTSD symptom severity — increasing during the session 7 setback (5 drinks/night) and decreasing as PTSD symptoms improved, ultimately normalizing to 0-1 drinks nightly without direct substance-focused intervention. This trajectory supports the clinical formulation that alcohol use was secondary to PTSD (functionally maintained by avoidance/self-medication motivation) rather than representing an independent substance use disorder. PCP confirms October LFTs normalized (GGT 42 U/L, previously elevated at 68). Recommendation: Remove R/O AUD from diagnostic list. Continue monitoring; if alcohol use increases during maintenance phase, reassess for independent AUD diagnosis.

5. Chronic Pain — ADDITIONAL CONSIDERATION.
While not a psychiatric diagnosis, Alex's chronic cervical spine pain (C4-C6 sprain secondary to MVA) functions as a perpetuating factor for PTSD (somatic trauma reminder, functional limitation, stress-pain cycle) and warrants ongoing coordination with physical therapy. PT notes improvement from pain 7/10 to 3/10 and reports behavioral improvement (reduced dissociation during PT sessions since mid-September, consistent with CPT progress).
3. TREATMENT PROGRESS SUMMARY

Treatment Modality: Cognitive Processing Therapy (CPT), 12-session protocol with adaptations for comorbid presentations.
Treatment Period: July 25, 2025 — October 10, 2025 (12 sessions over 11.5 weeks).
Session Format: Individual, 60 minutes (session 12 extended to 75 minutes for termination), in-person.

Phase 1 — Stabilization and Engagement (Sessions 1-3):
Sessions 1-3 focused on psychoeducation, therapeutic alliance building, safety plan implementation, and initial CPT engagement. Alex demonstrated rapid understanding of the cognitive model and strong capacity for self-reflection. Key clinical activities included: PTSD psychoeducation and normalization of symptoms, safety plan development and review, introduction of ABC worksheets for thought monitoring, impact statement assignment, and initial stuck point identification. By session 3, three primary stuck points were identified: (1) 'The accident was my fault,' (2) 'The world is completely dangerous,' and (3) 'I am broken and will never be the person I was.' The impact statement (session 3) revealed deep themes of self-blame, loss of safety, loss of identity, and a nascent connection to childhood experiences of unpredictability. Alliance strengthened steadily from 7/10 to 8/10. Homework compliance was strong from the outset (ABC worksheets 5/7 days, impact statement completed). Alcohol remained at 3-4 drinks nightly during this phase.

Phase 2 — Active CPT Processing (Sessions 4-8):
Sessions 4-6 represented the most productive phase of CPT. Stuck point #1 (self-blame) was systematically challenged using Challenging Questions and Patterns of Problematic Thinking worksheets. Alex demonstrated excellent cognitive capacity for identifying thinking errors (jumping to conclusions, emotional reasoning, overgeneralization). A key breakthrough occurred in session 5 when Alex generated a balanced belief about safety ('The world has dangers but is not entirely dangerous') and connected current safety schemas to childhood experiences with father's drinking: 'I think my dad's drinking taught me to always expect the worst.' This insight represented the beginning of a deeper developmental formulation that enriched the CPT work beyond the index trauma. Session 6 (Trust module) produced another powerful connection: trust difficulties with father mapped onto current inability to trust other drivers, trust in own judgment, and trust in safety of relationships.

Session 7 marked a significant setback. Alex experienced a severe dissociative flashback while a passenger in Jordan's car when a truck passed closely. The episode lasted approximately 3 minutes and was followed by a panic attack. Alcohol escalated to 5 drinks that evening and 4-5 nightly for the subsequent week. Nightmares returned to 5/week. Passive SI returned ('What's the point if I'm just going to keep reliving this'). Alliance was strained (7/10) as Alex questioned therapy efficacy. Session 7 was redirected from CPT protocol to crisis stabilization, grounding, motivational interviewing for alcohol, and safety plan update. CPT processing was paused.

Session 8 represented a careful bridge between stabilization and CPT resumption. Assessment scores showed the expected setback dip (PCL-5: 60, PHQ-9: 18, AUDIT: 16). The 'before and after therapy' comparison exercise proved effective in combating demoralization — Alex acknowledged gains: 'I guess I'm not completely back to square one. I know what stuck points are now and I caught myself in one.' CPT Power/Control module was gently introduced, connecting the concept of powerlessness to both the MVA (being trapped) and childhood (hiding during parental arguments).

Phase 3 — Integration and Growth (Sessions 9-12):
Sessions 9-12 demonstrated accelerated progress following the setback recovery. Session 9 was marked by a significant behavioral milestone: Alex rode the bus alone for the first time since the MVA, providing powerful behavioral evidence against the powerlessness stuck point. The Esteem module (sessions 9-10) facilitated a shift from 'I am broken' to 'I am changed but growing,' with Alex's return to creative work (a charcoal drawing brought to session 10) serving as both therapeutic evidence and identity reintegration. Session 10's artwork — depicting 'the space between the crash and now' — represented a clinically significant moment of post-traumatic growth, with Alex articulating: 'I'm not the designer I was before the accident, but maybe I'm becoming something different.'

Session 11 produced the most profound insight of treatment when Alex independently connected their current protective withdrawal pattern to the childhood caretaking role: 'I've been protecting people from my pain the way I used to protect Isabella from Dad's yelling.' This spontaneous integration of childhood schema into the post-trauma formulation — without clinician prompting — demonstrated internalization of CPT skills at the highest level. The Intimacy module was completed with renewed relational engagement (a meaningful conversation with Jordan where both cried and Alex 'didn't feel numb' for the first time).

Session 12 (final CPT session, extended to 75 minutes) included the revised impact statement, which demonstrated transformative cognitive change: 'The accident didn't break me. It broke open something that was already cracked from childhood, and now I'm putting it back together differently.' Relapse prevention planning, driving exposure initiation (parking lot with Jordan), and transition to maintenance phase were completed.

5. SUBSTANCE USE ASSESSMENT

Substance of Concern: Alcohol

Trajectory Across Treatment:

Intake (July 2025): 3-4 drinks nightly (craft beer/wine), primarily as sleep aid and anxiolytic. AUDIT: 18 (harmful use zone). Represented significant increase from pre-MVA baseline (1-2 drinks per weekend, social use only). Using alcohol to 'quiet my mind' and facilitate sleep. Partner Jordan expressed concern and noted pattern reminiscent of Alex's father's history.

Sessions 1-3: Maintained at 3-4 drinks nightly. Psychoeducation provided on alcohol-PTSD interaction. Not addressed as primary treatment target — clinical decision to focus on PTSD with hypothesis that alcohol use was secondary.

Session 4: Slight decrease to 2-3 drinks nightly, coinciding with initial PTSD symptom improvement. AUDIT: 14 (hazardous use). No direct alcohol intervention — decrease appeared to track with symptom improvement, supporting secondary use hypothesis.

Sessions 5-6: Continued at 2-3 drinks nightly, then stabilized at 2 drinks nightly. Gradual decrease without direct intervention.

Session 7 (setback): Escalated to 5 drinks on the night of the flashback episode, then 4-5 drinks nightly for the subsequent week. This escalation was the most concerning alcohol event in treatment and prompted direct intervention. Motivational interviewing was conducted, and psychoeducation on alcohol exacerbation of nightmares and hyperarousal was reinforced. Alex demonstrated ambivalence: 'I know the drinking is making it worse but it's the only thing that shuts my brain off.'

Session 8: Reduced to 3 drinks nightly (down from 5 but above pre-setback level). AUDIT: 16 (harmful use). Pre-sleep breathing routine introduced as behavioral replacement for alcohol-as-sleep-aid. PCP coordination: elevated GGT (68 U/L) noted on August labs.

Sessions 9-11: Progressive reduction from 2 drinks (session 9) to 1-2 drinks (session 10) to 1 drink most nights with 2 alcohol-free nights (session 11). Pre-sleep breathing routine implemented consistently. No direct substance-focused intervention beyond ongoing monitoring.

Session 12: 0-1 drinks nightly. AUDIT: 8 (at low-risk threshold). October LFTs normalized (GGT 42 U/L). Alcohol use pattern approximates social/moderate drinking.

Clinical Formulation: Alex's alcohol use pattern across treatment strongly supports the formulation that the increased drinking was functionally secondary to PTSD — specifically serving as an avoidance-based coping mechanism for intrusive symptoms, hyperarousal, and insomnia. Evidence for this formulation includes: (1) temporal correlation between PTSD severity and alcohol use at all four assessment timepoints; (2) alcohol escalation specifically during the session 7 setback and rapid reduction as PTSD symptoms improved; (3) normalization of alcohol use without substance-specific treatment (no formal AUD intervention, no 12-step referral, no motivational enhancement therapy protocol); and (4) Alex's stated function for alcohol use ('quiet my mind,' facilitate sleep) was progressively replaced by CPT coping skills and behavioral sleep hygiene.

Recommendation: The R/O AUD has been resolved (see Diagnostic Update). Continue monitoring alcohol use during maintenance phase as a sentinel indicator of PTSD symptom recurrence. If alcohol use increases above 2 drinks nightly on a sustained basis (>2 weeks), reassess for independent AUD and consider substance-specific referral. Alex's family history of AUD (father, paternal grandfather) represents an ongoing risk factor warranting longitudinal monitoring.

Cannabis: Use decreased from 2x/week (intake) to approximately 1x/week by session 12, with Alex reporting less perceived need. Cannabis use was not a primary treatment focus. Continue monitoring.

Caffeine: Reduced from 3-4 cups/day to 2 cups/day as sleep improved and fatigue decreased. No clinical concern.
6. UPDATED TREATMENT GOALS — MAINTENANCE PHASE (SMART FORMAT)
The following goals are established for the maintenance phase of treatment (biweekly sessions, estimated 2-4 months), based on current clinical status and remaining areas for growth.
7. INTERVENTION PLAN — MAINTENANCE PHASE

Modality: Individual psychotherapy, biweekly (every 2 weeks), 50-minute sessions, in-person at Lakeshore Behavioral Health Center. Transition to monthly sessions after 2 months if clinically indicated.

Evidence Base: Maintenance phase following CPT is supported by VA/DoD Clinical Practice Guidelines for PTSD (2023), which recommend continued monitoring and booster sessions following active treatment, particularly for clients with comorbid presentations and partial remission.

Therapeutic Interventions:
1. CPT Skill Reinforcement and Relapse Prevention: Continue to monitor stuck points and cognitive distortions. Review and practice Challenging Beliefs Worksheets as needed. Reinforce gains using real-world experiences (driving exposure, social engagement) as in-session evidence. Review relapse prevention plan at each session.

2. Graduated Exposure for Driving: Support systematic, self-paced driving exposure hierarchy: (a) parking lot driving with partner (initiated session 12), (b) residential streets with low traffic, (c) moderate-traffic roads during low-traffic hours, (d) highway driving with partner, (e) independent highway driving. Process driving experiences in session using CPT framework (stuck points activated, balanced thoughts generated). Coordinate with potential future driving rehabilitation specialist if needed.

3. Behavioral Sleep Optimization: Continue pre-sleep breathing routine. Monitor sleep quality and nightmares. Coordinate with PCP regarding trazodone taper if sleep normalizes. If nightmares persist or worsen, consider Image Rehearsal Therapy (IRT) as adjunct, which has Level A evidence for trauma-related nightmares.

4. Relational Maintenance: Support continued relational reconnection with Jordan. Process intimacy-related stuck points as they arise. Provide couples therapy referral if Alex and Jordan express interest (referral list provided at session 12). Monitor for relapse of emotional withdrawal.

5. Identity Integration: Support continued creative engagement as vehicle for post-traumatic growth. Process evolving self-concept ('changed but growing' versus 'broken'). Explore intersection of Mexican-American heritage, non-binary identity, and post-trauma identity in a culturally responsive manner.

6. Substance Use Monitoring: Brief alcohol use check-in at each session (quantity/frequency). If use exceeds 2 drinks/night for >2 consecutive weeks, implement SBIRT (Screening, Brief Intervention, Referral to Treatment) protocol. Monitor cannabis use.

Medication Coordination Plan:
- Maintain sertraline 100mg for minimum 6 additional months (PCP and clinician agreement per October 14 coordination call)
- Trazodone 50mg: PCP to consider tapering to PRN basis at next visit given sleep improvement. If nightmares recur, prazosin 1mg at bedtime remains available option.
- No new psychotropic medications anticipated unless clinical trajectory changes
- PCP appointments scheduled routinely (next: November 12, 2025)
- Clinician will communicate with PCP at minimum every 8 weeks or as clinically indicated

1. Driving Avoidance as Barrier to Session Attendance: Alex currently relies on bus, rideshare, or partner for transportation. Inclement weather or schedule conflicts could disrupt attendance during biweekly maintenance phase. Mitigation: Offer telehealth option for sessions when in-person attendance is not feasible; continue supporting driving exposure as a treatment goal that will ultimately resolve this barrier.

2. Occupational Demands: As work performance improves and accommodations are reduced (deadline extension already decreased from 25% to 15%), Alex may face increased occupational stress. Mitigation: Monitor work-related stress in session; coordinate with HR accommodations as needed; support assertive communication with supervisor about workload.

3. Insurance/Financial: Auto insurance claim is pending and may create stress related to IME process, potential adversarial evaluations, and litigation. The September 2025 IME was processed in session 8 — Alex experienced it as invalidating despite the examiner's professional conduct. Mitigation: Process insurance-related stress in session; maintain clear clinical documentation that supports treatment necessity and progress; provide treatment records as needed with client consent.

4. Setback Vulnerability: The session 7 setback demonstrated that in-vivo trauma triggers (truck proximity while in vehicle) can rapidly reactivate symptoms. As Alex increases driving exposure, the likelihood of encountering trigger situations increases. Mitigation: Relapse prevention plan in place; ensure Alex has coping strategies readily accessible during driving; process any setbacks using CPT framework rather than interpreting as treatment failure.

5. Anniversary Reaction: The MVA anniversary (November 15, 2025) falls within the maintenance phase and may trigger symptom exacerbation. Mitigation: Address proactively in the session preceding the anniversary; develop specific coping plan; schedule a session close to the anniversary date.

6. Partner Fatigue: Jordan has been a primary support throughout treatment but expressed exhaustion in the collateral interview. Prolonged caregiving burden may strain the relationship. Mitigation: Couples therapy referral provided; encourage Alex to express appreciation and reciprocate support; monitor relationship quality in session.
